title: Relative Motion
content:
Relative Motion

Relative motion describes how the position and velocity of an object appear different to observers in different frames of reference.

Frames of Reference

A frame of reference is a coordinate system from which motion is observed and measured. Motion is always described relative to a chosen frame.

  • Stationary Frame: The ground is often used as a reference.
  • Moving Frame: A car, train, or airplane can be a moving frame.

Relative Velocity

The velocity of an object relative to a particular frame is given by:

$$\vec{v}_{A/B} = \vec{v}_A - \vec{v}_B$$

Where $\vec{v}_{A/B}$ is the velocity of A relative to B.

Example: If a boat moves east at 5 m/s relative to the river, and the river flows north at 3 m/s relative to the ground, the boat's velocity relative to the ground is the vector sum of the two velocities.

Example Problem

A plane flies at 200 km/h north relative to the air. The wind blows east at 50 km/h. What is the plane's velocity relative to the ground?

  • Use the Pythagorean theorem: $v = \sqrt{200^2 + 50^2}$
  • Direction: $ heta = an^{-1}(50/200)$ east of north
